coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquids as precursors for highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots used for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Andong
Zhao, Chuanqi
Li, Meng
Ren, Jinsong
Qu, Xiaogang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (C-Dots) have attracted much attention in recent years due to their low cost, ready scalability, excellent chemical stability, biocompatibility and multicolor luminescence. Here, we report a facile strategy for producing highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots (C-Dots) by using different ionic liquids (ILs). Intriguingly, the surface-different C-Dots show different selectivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example which shows that ILs are excellent precursors for producing luminescent nanomaterial used for detection of different metal ions. The resultant nitrogen-doped C-Dots are highly photoluminescent and can be used for multicolor bioimaging. Most notable, by taking different ILs as precursors, we obtain surface-different C-Dots, which can be directly used for selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ without any modification. These C-Dots based sensors exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity and the sensing process can be easily accomplished with one-step rapid operation. More importantly, compared with other method using QDs, organic dyes and organic solvent, this strategy is much more eco-friendly. This work may offer a new approach for developing low cost and sensitive C-Dots-based sensors for biological and environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquids
Carbon dots
Detection
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low- and (b) high-magnification TEM images of the C-Dots 1 and (c) the corresponding size distribution histogram. (d) Low- and (e) high-magnification TEM images of the C-Dots 2 and (f) the corresponding size distribution histogram. Scale bar is 50nm for low-magnification TEM images and 2nm for high-magnification TEM images.
UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra (with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 300nm to 500nm) of C-Dots 1(a) and C-Dots 2 (b), respectively.

(a) and (b) The difference in photoluminescence intensity of C-Dots dispersion between the blank and solutions containing different metal ions (excitation at 340nm; [Mn+]=100μM, F 0 and F are C-Dots photoluminescence intensities at 405 (C-Dots1) and 390 (C-Dots2) nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively). Quenching plot of C-Dots1 and C-Dots2 PL by Cu2+(excitation at 340nm; F 0 and F are C-Dots 1 fluorescence intensities at 405nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively)/Fe3+ (excitation at 340nm; F 0 and F are C-Dots 2 fluorescence intensities at 405nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively).

Fluorescence microscopy images of Hela cells labeled with the C-Dots 1: (a) excitation by UV light (330–385nm); (b) excitation by blue light (450–480nm); (c) excitation by green light (510–550nm); and C-Dots 2: (d) excitation by UV light (330–385nm); (e) excitation by blue light (450–480nm); (f) excitation by green light (510–550nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of synthetizing surface-different C-Dots and their application in label-free detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+.
Effect of ILs content on the QY.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Financial support was provided by 973 (2011CB936004, 2012CB720602), and NSFC (21210002, 91213302).

head.ce:title: Ionic liquids as precursors for highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots used for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Division of Biological Inorganic Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Division of Biological Inorganic Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report a facile strategy for producing highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots (C-Dots) by using different ionic liquids (ILs). Intriguingly, the surface-different C-Dots show different selectivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+.

Carbon nanodots (C-Dots) have attracted much attention in recent years due to their low cost, ready scalability, excellent chemical stability, biocompatibility and multicolor luminescence. Here, we report a facile strategy for producing highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots (C-Dots) by using different ionic liquids (ILs). Intriguingly, the surface-different C-Dots show different selectivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example which shows that ILs are excellent precursors for producing luminescent nanomaterial used for detection of different metal ions. The resultant nitrogen-doped C-Dots are highly photoluminescent and can be used for multicolor bioimaging. Most notable, by taking different ILs as precursors, we obtain surface-different C-Dots, which can be directly used for selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ without any modification. These C-Dots based sensors exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity and the sensing process can be easily accomplished with one-step rapid operation. More importantly, compared with other method using QDs, organic dyes and organic solvent, this strategy is much more eco-friendly. This work may offer a new approach for developing low cost and sensitive C-Dots-based sensors for biological and environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As newcomers to the carbon nanomaterials family, photoluminescent carbon dots (C-Dots) have attracted much attention because of their remarkable advances in high resistance to photobleaching, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [1,2]. Bearing these attractive merits, C-Dots have recently been regarded as one of the most promising nanomaterials for bioimaging, fluorescence sensors, drug delivery and optoelectronic devices, etc. [3–11]. To date, many methods have been used to synthesize C-Dots such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, combustion/thermal oxidation, supported synthesis, or microwave treatment and so on [1,5,12–15]. Among them, microwave methods appear to be more feasible because they are simple, fast and energy efficient.

Although some simple and inexpensive methods for preparation of C-Dots have been recently reported, most of the resultant C-Dots suffer from low quantum yields (QYs) (always less than 10%), which severely limits their availability for practical applications [16–18]. On the other hand, attention has also been paid to diversifying the surface group of C-Dots for post-functionalization and enlarging their application in diverse fields. Thus, the development of simple, cost-effective and environment-friendly method for large scale synthesis of highly luminescent, surface-different C-Dots remains challenging.

Ionic liquids (ILs) are neoteric materials, which have garnered attention from both academia and industry during the last decade [19]. As “green” solvents, ILs have been used in diverse aspects, for example, in chemical synthesis, liquid/liquid extraction, electrochemistry, catalysis [19–22]. Currently, ILs are being investigated for a variety of bio-applications including biocatalysis [23], protein stabilization [24] and nucleic acids storage [25–27]. Besides as solvents, many groups have used task-specified ILs to build functional nanomaterials [28]. Recent reports showed that nitrogen-containing materials or molecules could be used for preparation of high photoluminescent C-Dots [29–31]. Most ILs possesses organic nitrogen-containing bulky cation and these may render them ideal molecular precursors for luminescent C-Dots.

Herein, we report a facile and straightforward preparative route toward photoluminescent C-Dots by directly microwaving hydrated ILs. The resultant nitrogen-doped C-Dots were highly luminescent for cell-imaging. Most interestingly, by taking different ILs as precursors, we obtained surface-different C-Dots, which can be used for label-free detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ respectively, with high selectivity and sensitivity (Scheme 1 ).

ILs ([BMIM][Br] and [BMIM][BF4]) were prepared according to the reported literatures [19]. Standard stock solutions of Cu2+/Fe3+ were prepared with CuSO4/FeCl3 in double-distilled water. Different concentrations of Cu2+/Fe3+ were obtained by diluting standard stock solutions. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. All experiments were carried out in aqueous PBS buffer (10mM sodium phosphate, pH=6.5) unless stated otherwise. All water used to prepare buffer solutions was obtained by using a Milli-Q water system. Other metal salts used in this work including MgCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, NiCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, Al(NO3)3, AgNO3, and Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and used without further purification.

UV–vis spectroscopy was carried out with a JASCO V-550 UV/vis spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements were carried out using a JASCO FP-6500 spectrofluorometer. Atomic-force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed using Nanoscope V multimode atomic force microscope (Veeco Instruments, USA). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded using an FEI TECNAI G220 high-resolution transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV. XPS measurement was performed on an ESCALAB-MKII spectrometer (VG Co., United Kingdom) with Al Kα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. FT-IR characterization was carried out on a BRUKE Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer (2cm−1).

Samples were prepared by dissolving different amounts of ILs ([BMIM][Br] and [BMIM][BF4]) to 1mL water. The samples were then put into a domestic microwave oven (650W) with reaction for 2min. The obtained brownish-black solid powder was dissolved with 10mL water. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 10min and then dialyzed against ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 48h to remove the excess precursors and resulting small molecules. The as-prepared C-Dots used as stock. The C-Dots made from [BMIM][Br] and [BMIM][BF4] was named as C-Dots 1 and C-Dots 2, respectively.

The quantum yield (QY) of C-Dots was measured according to the established procedure. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield is 0.54 at 360nm) was chosen as a standard. Absolute values were calculated using the standard reference sample that had a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value. In order to minimize absorption effects absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (360nm).

The detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ was performed in PBS (10mM, pH=6.5) buffer solution. In a typical assay, C-Dots (10μL) dispersion was added into PBS buffer (400μL), followed by the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+/Fe3+. The FL spectra were recorded after reaction for 5min. The selectivity for Cu2+/Fe3+ was confirmed by adding other metal ions stock solutions instead of Cu2+/Fe3+ in a similar way. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

The HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Gibco) in culture discs at 37°C incubator with 5% CO2. C-Dots were administered into the cell culture. After an incubation of 6h, cells were washed two times with 1× PBS buffer and the fluorescence images were captured using an Olympus BX-51 optical system microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with an Olympus EVOLT E-500 digital camera.

MTT assays were used to probe cellular viability. Cells were seeded at a density of 5000 cells/well (100μL total volume/well) in 96-well assay plates. After 24h of incubation, the as-prepared C-Dots, at the indicated concentrations, were added for further incubation of 48h. To determine toxicity, we added 10μL MTT solution (BBI) to each well of the microtiter plate, and the plate was incubated in the CO2 incubator for an additional 4h. Then, the cells were lysed by the addition of 100μL of DMSO. Absorbance values of formazan were determined with a Bio-Rad model-680 microplate reader at 490nm (corrected for background absorbance at 630nm). Six replicates were done for each treatment group.

In this paper, we used two ILs: 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([BMIM][Br]) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([BMIM][BF4]). C-Dots made from hydrated [BMIM][Br] was named as C-Dots 1 while C-Dots derived from hydrated [BMIM][BF4] was named as C-Dots 2 (ILs are dissolved in water with 40% content). As shown in Fig. 1 , transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images clearly revealed that the C-Dots 1/C-Dots 2 were monodisperse nanoparticles with a narrow size distribution of 2–6nm in diameter. The diameter distribution was further confirmed by AFM (Fig. S1). The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of C-Dots 1 and C-Dots 2 both showed lattice spacing of 0.210, which was close to the (102) facet of graphite (Fig. 1) [32].

FT-IR spectra were employed to identify the functional groups present in the as-prepared C-Dots. For C-Dots 1, the revealed peaks at 3090, 2959, 2833 and 1578cm−1, were corresponded to the imidazole or imidazole structures (Fig. S2a), which indicated many imidazole groups maintaining on the surface of C-Dots 1 after microwaving. As for C-Dots 2, the broad and intense peak centered at 3400cm−1 exhibited the existence of a large number of residual hydroxyl groups (Fig. S2b). These results were further evidenced by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The intensity of “C–N” for C-Dots 1 was higher than C-Dots 2, while the intensity of “C–O” for C-Dots 2 was higher than C-Dots 1 (Fig. S3). In addition, XPS results indicated that both the C-Dots were well nitrogen-doped.

Combined with the above analysis of FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra, we can conclude that C-Dots 1 and C-Dots 2 were surface-different. C-Dots 1 was rich of imidazole group on the surface, while C-Dots 2 was hydroxyl-rich on the surface. As illustrated in Scheme 1, the two ILs were different in anions (Br− and [BF4]−), with the same cation ([BMIM]+). It was reported that microwaves directly excited polar molecules (dipole relaxation) and ions (ionic conduction) [33,34]. Therefore, the different polarity between [BMIM][Br] and [BMIM][BF4] might lead to their different carbonated content when microwaving. Furthermore, the ILs used here were water-containing, in which Br− and [BF4]− possessed different radius and ionic conductive properties [35], which might be another contributing factors to the formation of surface-different C-Dots.

The influence of pH on C-Dots in the range 2–12 was also studied. The results showed that the maximum fluorescence was observed at pH=4.5 for C-Dots 1 and pH=6.5 for C-Dots 2 (Fig. S5). Both the two C-Dots exhibited good fluorescence over a wide range (pH=2–8), making them of a valuable in biological applications.

It is well known that Cu2+ and Fe3+ are very important in metabolic processes, being indispensable for all living systems [36,37]. However, both its deficiency and excess from the normal permissible limit can induce serious disorders. Therefore, qualitative and quantitative determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is crucial in clinical, medical and environmental. Recent years, metal-based quantum dots (QDs) have become one of the most extensively optical sensing nanomaterials [38]. However, they suffer from the serious toxicity of the heavy metal elements. Therefore, the development of novel nontoxic optical sensor is highly desired.

We next explored the feasibility of using such C-Dots for ions detection. As seen in Fig. 3 a, Cu2+ seriously decreased the fluorescence of C-Dots 1 and only weak fluorescence signals changed for other metal ions listed in the graph under the same experiment conditions. Furthermore, the selectivity of the C-Dots in the presence of possible interference ions was evaluated considering the cross reactivity (Fig. S6). As previously reported, Cu2+ quenched the fluorescence of C-Dots 1 through electron or energy transfer [31]. The high selectivity of C-Dots 1 for Cu2+ might be due to that Cu2+ had a higher thermodynamic affinity and faster chelating process with “N” in imidazole group existed on C-Dots 1 surface [39]. On the contrary, only Fe3+ effectively decreased the fluorescence of C-Dots 2 (Fig. 3b). Similarly, Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of C-Dots 2 through electron or energy transfer. Previous reports showed that hydroxyl exhibited good binding affinity for Fe3+ [36]. Thus, the Fe3+-selective property of C-Dots 2 could be attributed to its hydroxyl-rich surface as characterized above. The above results indicated that C-Dots 1 was highly sensitive to Cu2+, while C-Dots 2 was highly sensitive to Fe3+ and both the two C-Dots exhibited good selectivity over other metal ions. The detection limit (taken to be three times the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements) was estimated to be 5nM for Cu2+ and 20nM for Fe3+, respectively. This detection sensitivity of Cu2+/Fe3+ was comparable or even superior to some reported methods that required chemical modification of the sensor [40]. The above results clearly demonstrated that C-Dots 1 was a good Cu2+ sensor and C-Dots 2 was a good Fe3+ sensor.

Microscopy studies indicated that the two C-Dots were well cell-imaging agents with good biocompatibility. Fig. 4 showed microscopy images of HeLa cells labeled with the two C-Dots after a co-incubation of 6h. Apparently, C-Dots could enter into the HeLa cells and gave bright fluorescent light. For further biological applications, MTT assays were carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the C-Dots to HeLa cells. As expected, the viability of the cells declined less than 10% upon addition of both the two C-Dots even up to 0.25mgmL−1 (Fig. S7). Thus, the prepared C-Dots can be considered to be nontoxic, further indicating that the C-Dots were of well biocompatibility.

In summary, we report a novel preparative route toward fluorescent C-Dots by taking ILs as sources. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example which shows that C-Dots made from different ILs can be used to detect different ions. The resultant nitrogen-doped C-Dots are highly photoluminescent and can be used for multicolor bioimaging. Most notable, by taking different ILs as precursors, we obtain surface-different C-Dots which can be directly used for detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ without any modification. These C-Dots based sensors exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity and the sensing process can be easily accomplished with one-step rapid operation. More importantly, compared with other method using QDs, organic dyes and organic solvent, this novel strategy is much more environmentally friendly. We expect that this strategy may offer a new approach for developing low cost and sensitive sensors in biological and environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2013.10.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
